paper.title: Counterculture Stars: Slow and Retrograde Stars with Low-Alpha Disk Abundances

extraction.summary: The paper studies ~69 slow and retrograde stars with thin-disk-like abundances as bound kinematic outliers of the Galactic thin disk. No object is presented as possibly unbound from the Milky Way; the Hills mechanism subsection explicitly limits its scope to bound stars. All objects are excluded from HVS candidacy.

candidate_groups_considered.description: The full sample of 69 slow and retrograde stars with thin-disk-like abundances selected from APOGEE DR17 (Table tab:key / table-002).
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper treats these stars as bound kinematic outliers of the thin disk and explores six mechanisms that could alter their orbits while remaining bound to the Galaxy. The Hills mechanism discussion explicitly states the authors seek 'only the rate of production of bound stars.' No object in the sample is presented as possibly unbound, escaping, or a hypervelocity star candidate.

source_refs.context: Abstract describing the sample as kinematical outliers of the thin disk or elemental abundance outliers of the halo, not unbound stars.
Selection of the final sample of 69 slow and retrograde stars with thin-disk-like abundances.
Hills mechanism section explicitly limiting the analysis to bound stars: 'We seek to estimate only the rate of production of bound stars.'

candidate_groups_considered.description: The nine high-metallicity stars flagged as most consistent with the Hills mechanism, which is traditionally associated with hypervelocity star production.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: Although the Hills mechanism is a known channel for producing hypervelocity stars, this paper explicitly focuses on lower-velocity ejections that remain bound to the Galaxy. The nine high-metallicity stars are discussed as bound candidates with eccentric orbits, not as unbound or escaping objects.

source_refs.context: Hills mechanism section stating the focus is on 'lower velocity ejections' and the rate of 'bound stars.'
Identification of nine high-metallicity stars as consistent with the Hills mechanism, described as being on eccentric rather than unbound orbits.
